MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT
Off Calendar Mar-19-2020 Text Ruling
MINI MINUTES:
Appearances:
Kevin F. Barrett, esq. (510-540-4881) of Bremer Whyte Brown & O'Meara, LLP for interested party via court call.

DISCUSSED (as fully reported in the reporter's transcript). The Court having reviewed the pleadings and having heard oral argument rules as follows:

DEFENDANT DANIEL MENZIES' MOTION TO STRIKE 1ST AMENDED COMPLAINT:

Defendant Daniel Garald Menzies's motion to strike portions of plaintiff's first amended complaint is granted without leave to amend. In order to state a prima facie claim for punitive damages, a complaint must set forth the elements stated in the general punitive damage statute, Civil Code section 3294, including allegations that the defendant has been guilty of oppression, fraud, or malice. (Civ. Code 3294(a).) "Malice" is defined as conduct "intended by the defendant to cause injury to plaintiff, or despicable conduct that is carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard for the rights or safety of others." (Civ. Code 3294(c)(1).) Absent an intent to injure the plaintiff, malice requires more than a "willful and conscious" disregard of the plaintiff's interests; the additional component of "despicable conduct" must be found. (College Hospital, Inc. v. Superior Court (1994) 8 Cal.4th 704, 725.) Punitive damages are appropriate only if the defendant's actions are reprehensible, fraudulent, or in blatant violation of law or policy. Punitive damages are proper only when the tortious conduct rises to levels of extreme indifference to the plaintiff's rights, a level which decent citizens should not have to tolerate. (Pacific Gas and Electric Company v. Superior Court (2018) 24 Cal.App.5th 1150, 1170-76.)

Here, plaintiff's allegations that defendant was "not paying attention to traffic because [he was] using a cell phone while driving" (FAC 18) and was "looking down and checking, entering, tapping, and/or otherwise operating [his] cell phone" (FAC 27) do not rise to the level of despicable conduct carried on by the defendant with a willful and conscious disregard for the rights or safety of others required by Civ. Code 3294(c)(1). At most, Plaintiff's allegations show "mere carelessness or ignorance, which is insufficient to establish malice." (Pacific Gas and Electric Company, 24 Cal.App.5th at 1171; see Lackner v. North (2006) 135 Cal.App.4th 1188, 1209-1210 [evidence that snowboarder who struck skier was not "despicable" so as to support punitive damages claim; even if snowboarder was reckless in traveling at high speed in rest area without looking in the direction he was heading, he did not hit skier intentionally and attempted to avoid her]; see also Brousseau v. Jarrett (1977) 73 Cal.App.3d 864, 872 ["conclusory characterization of defendant's conduct as intentional, willful and fraudulent is a patently insufficient statement of 'oppression, fraud, or malice, express or implied,' within the meaning of section 3294."].)

Order signed after email verification by both counsel that order has been approved as to form.

Judge: Ethan P. Schulman, Clerk: S. Le, Court Reporter: Maria Torreano, CSR#8600, maria.torreano@gmail.com, Reported. =(302/EPS)
